title: Trump teleprompter aide made $100,000 betting on what Trump would say, reports say
gist: Trump's teleprompter operator, Gabriel Perez, allegedly made $100,000 on Kalshi's 'mention markets' by betting on words/phrases in Trump's speeches - sometimes adjusting bets mid-speech based on real-time knowledge of edits - illustrating a growing pattern of government-insider trading on federally regulated prediction markets that the CFTC has so far handled civilly rather than criminally.

insights.claim: Trump aide Gabriel Perez, who allegedly has 'final eyes on nearly all of the president's prepared remarks,' made approximately $100,000 on Kalshi's 'mention market' contracts betting on specific words or phrases Trump would say in speeches, and reportedly backed out of bets mid-speech when Trump skipped portions containing the word he had bet on.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: A White House staffer trading on real-time nonpublic knowledge of a president's remarks is a concrete case study of how mention-market contract types create insider-trading exposure unique to prediction markets versus traditional securities.
insights.locator: Sources say Perez typically has the final eyes

insights.claim: Kalshi itself flagged Perez's unusual activity, investigated, froze his funds, and referred the matter to the CFTC, but the CFTC alerted federal prosecutors in Manhattan, who declined to open a criminal investigation; Perez is reportedly in settlement talks with the CFTC (a civil, not criminal, resolution) and lost his White House job.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: The lack of criminal prosecution despite a clear-cut, self-reported insider case suggests weak deterrence against insider trading on prediction markets, a risk factor for the integrity and regulatory scrutiny of the asset class as it scales.
insights.locator: In this case, Kalshi (which bans this sort of activity)

themes.theme: A recurring pattern of government-insider bets

insights.claim: Prior cases include Gannon Ken Van Dyke, a US soldier who made $410,000 on Polymarket after using classified planning knowledge of the operation to capture Venezuela's Nicolas Maduro (arrested April 2026), and former Congressman George Santos, who allegedly bet on whether he would attend the State of the Union and then skipped it at the last minute to win the payout.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Establishes that the Perez case is not isolated but part of a recurring pattern of government insiders monetizing nonpublic knowledge on prediction markets, suggesting systemic monitoring gaps across platforms, not just Kalshi.
insights.locator: The classic example, of course, was Gannon Ken Van Dyke

insights.claim: The Trump White House issued a memo in March 2026 directing employees not to 'use nonpublic information to buy or sell these contracts,' an explicit acknowledgment of the insider-trading risk prediction markets pose to government staff even before the Perez case became public.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Shows the White House was already aware of the risk months before this specific case surfaced, raising questions about enforcement effectiveness of internal compliance policies for staff trading on prediction markets.
insights.quote: use nonpublic information to buy or sell these contracts
insights.locator: Even the Trump White House was concerned enough

themes.theme: Kalshi's regulatory strategy and market expansion

insights.claim: Kalshi is pursuing federal preemption through the CFTC to override state gambling regulators, with the CFTC suing states including Kentucky, Minnesota, Illinois, and Rhode Island to establish a single national regulatory standard for prediction markets that Kalshi would operate under.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Kalshi's business model depends on winning the federal-preemption legal fight against state gambling law; the outcome of these state lawsuits is a binary catalyst for whether Kalshi can operate nationally under commodities-law framing rather than a patchwork of state gambling restrictions.
insights.locator: To head this off, Kalshi has sought federal protection

insights.claim: Prediction markets are expanding contract types like 'mention markets' beyond politics and sports into areas such as drug trials and flight cancellations, and have been advertising heavily during the World Cup, broadening both retail engagement and the potential surface area for insider-information abuse as the category grows.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: As prediction markets expand into more information-sensitive domains (clinical trials, corporate earnings-call content), the number of insiders with tradeable nonpublic knowledge grows correspondingly, an underappreciated compliance and reputational risk for the sector.
insights.locator: Prediction markets, which have invested heavily in advertising
